title: Resolving Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator with Union Types

statement.markdown: Implement a generic math calculator function `calculate<T>(num1: T, num2: T): T` that takes two numbers as input and returns their sum. However, when using the function with different numeric types (e.g. `calculate(10, 20)` or `calculate(10.5, 20.5)`), TypeScript throws a type error. Fix the implementation to resolve type inference for union types.
statement.inputFormat: Two numeric inputs of the same type (e.g. integers or floating-point numbers)
statement.outputFormat: The sum of the two input numbers
statement.notes: The function should handle both integer and floating-point arithmetic correctly.

hints.text: Use the `number` type union to handle both integers and floating-point numbers

editorial.title: Implementing Generic Math Calculator with Union Types
editorial.intuition: To resolve type inference for union types, we can use the `number` type union, which includes both integers and floating-point numbers.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible approach is to use the `any` type, but this will disable TypeScript's type checking.
editorial.betterSolution: Another approach is to use the `number` type union, which includes both integers and floating-point numbers.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the `number` type union and implement the function using template literal type syntax to ensure correct type inference.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We can use the `number` type union to handle both integers and floating-point numbers, and the template literal type syntax to ensure correct type inference.
commonMistakes: Using the `any` type to disable type checking
Not using the `number` type union to handle both integers and floating-point numbers

functionSignature.javascript: function calculate<T>(num1: T, num2: T): T
